en.extract:
Finn's weaver (Ploceus megarhynchus), also known as Finn's baya and yellow weaver is a weaver bird species native to the Ganges and Brahmaputra valleys in India and Nepal. Two subspecies are known; the nominate subspecies occurs in the Kumaon area and salimalii in the eastern Terai.
The species was described and given its binomial name by Allan Octavian Hume based on a specimen obtained at Kaladhungi near Nainital.


Etymology

The species was called "Eastern baya" by Oates in 1889 and called Finn's baya after Frank Finn by E. C. Stuart Baker in 1925.


Distribution and habitat
Finn's weaver was first observed in Shuklaphanta National Park in May 1996 and is a regular summer visitor.


Behaviour and ecology
Finn's weaver breeds from May to September. It builds a nest on top of trees or in reeds. The nest is different in structure from the other weaver species found in India, but as in other weavers, woven from thin strips of leaves and reeds. This species lines the entire inside of the nest, unlike the other weavers, which line only the floor of the nest. Males strip the leaves of the nest tree, making the globular nests clearly visible.


References


Further reading


External links
Finn's weaver -  Species text in Weaver Watch.

'''Finn's weaver''' ('''''Ploceus megarhynchus'''''), also known as '''Finn's baya''' and '''yellow weaver''' is a [[Ploceidae|weaver bird]] species native to the [[Ganges]] and [[Brahmaputra]] valleys in [[India]] and [[Nepal]]. Two subspecies are known; the [[nominate subspecies]] occurs in the Kumaon area and ''salimalii'' in the eastern [[Terai]].

The species was described and given its binomial name by [[Allan Octavian Hume]] based on a specimen obtained at Kaladhungi near [[Nainital]].

== Etymology ==
[[File:PloceusMegarhynchusSmit.jpg|thumb|Illustration of Finn's weaver by Joseph Smit (1901)]]
The species was called "Eastern baya" by [[Eugene W. Oates|Oates]] in 1889 and called Finn's baya after [[Frank Finn]] by [[E. C. Stuart Baker]] in 1925.<ref>{{cite book |author=Oates, E.W. |year=1890 |publisher=Taylor and Francis|place=London |title=Fauna of British India |volume=Birds. Volume 2 |pages=177–178 |chapter=''Ploceus megarhynchus''. The Eastern Baya |chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsindia02oaterich#page/176/mode/1up}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=Baker, E.C.S. |year=1925 |title=Fauna of British India |volume=Birds. Volume 3 |edition=Second|publisher=Taylor and Francis |place=London |pages=69–70 |chapter=''Ploceus megarhynchus'' Finn's baya |chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds3/BakerFBI3#page/n90/mode/1up}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title=Finn's baya (''Ploceus megarhynchus'' Hume) |author=Abdulali, H. |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |year=1954 |volume=51 |issue=1 |pages=200–204 |url=https://archive.org/details/journalofbo5119521953bomb/page/200/mode/2up}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author1=Beolens, B. |author2=Watkins, M. |year= 2003|title=Whose bird? Men and women commemorated in the common names of birds |publisher=Christopher Helm}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
Finn's weaver was first observed in [[Shuklaphanta National Park]] in May 1996 and is a regular summer visitor.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Basnet, H. |name-list-style=amp |author2=Thakuri, D.C. |author3=Bhetwal, K. |author4=Joshi, D. |author5=Poudyal, L.P. |year=2019 |title=Recent records of threatened birds in the Sukilaphanta Grassland, Suklaphanta National Park, Nepal, with particular focus on White-throated Bushchat ''Saxicola insignis'' and Finn's Weaver ''Ploceus megarhynchus'' |journal=Birding Asia |issue=32 |pages=93–96 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/340730965}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
Finn's weaver breeds from May to September.<ref name="birdlife-int">BirdLife International (2009) Species factsheet: ''Ploceus megarhynchus''. [http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=8547&m=0 BirdLife International: ''Yellow Weaver'']</ref> It builds a nest on top of trees or in reeds. The nest is different in structure from the other weaver species found in India, but as in other weavers, woven from thin strips of leaves and reeds. This species lines the entire inside of the nest, unlike the other weavers, which line only the floor of the nest. Males strip the leaves of the nest tree, making the globular nests clearly visible.{{cn|date=February 2022}}

* Saha, S.S. (1976) Occurrence of Finn's Baya (''Ploceus megarhynchus'' Hume) in Darrang District, Assam. Journal of the Bombay Natural History 73(3): 527–529.
